claim: Keep systems simple, starting with prompt engineering, then workflows, then agents only if necessary; minimize tools and consider agents only for open-ended problems
actionable: Start with prompt engineering alone; upgrade to workflows for predefined steps; use agents only when you cannot enumerate task steps ahead of time
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Models improve exponentially (autonomy doubling every 7 months), so architectures built today will become obsolete and require constant rearchitecting
actionable: Design systems for rapid iteration and model upgrades; don't bake rigid assumptions into your harness; expect to rebuild your application every 6-12 months
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: The bitter lesson: general methods leveraging compute beat complex, hand-crafted methods; apply this to AI systems by avoiding rigid structure and letting models improve simplify your harness
actionable: Strip away assumptions and structure from your harness as models improve; prefer simple, compute-hungry approaches over hand-optimized complexity
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Context rot: models degrade in performance as context length increases; effective context windows are often much smaller than technical limits (e.g., far less than 1M tokens)
actionable: Do not use full available context window; test agent performance across different context lengths; implement context reduction even with large windows
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Three-part context engineering playbook: (1) reduce context by compacting/pruning old tool calls and summarizing conversation history, (2) offload tool results to file systems and use few atomic tools (bash, file system) instead of many, (3) isolate heavy tasks to sub-agents
actionable: Implement context pruning after N turns; use summarization when nearing context limits; offload tool outputs to persistent storage; route heavy tasks to sub-agents
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Expand agent action space via a small set of atomic tools (like bash and file system) rather than binding many tools to the model; this reduces token overhead while maintaining capability
actionable: Instead of binding 50+ tools to your model, bind 2-5 atomic tools (bash, file system, web search) and let the agent script combinations through those
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Cloud Code uses a 70% model, 30% scaffolding ratio; as models improve, that scaffolding becomes redundant and must be stripped away (not added to)
actionable: Track what fraction of your agent's success is due to model vs. harness; plan to simplify harness as models improve rather than adding guardrails
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Workflows are better for predefined, predictable steps (testing, migrations); agents better for open-ended, adaptive tasks (research, coding); agents can be embedded in workflows
actionable: Use workflows for known sequences (PR testing, data migration); use agents for tasks where the next step depends on previous results (debugging, research); combine both in larger systems
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Evaluation must shift from static benchmarks (which saturate quickly) to in-app user feedback and production traces; this is the modern best practice across Claude Code and Manus
actionable: Set up tracing/logging of all agent runs; collect in-app thumbs up/down feedback; roll bad examples into eval sets; refresh eval data continuously rather than relying on fixed benchmarks
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Test system robustness against model capacity; evaluate against low, mid, and high-capacity models to ensure the harness is future-proof and doesn't bottleneck improvement
actionable: Run your eval suite against Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus; performance should increase monotonically; if not, your harness is limiting model improvement
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Avoid rushing to fine-tune; frontier models improve so fast that by the time you collect data and train, the frontier model may already include the capability (e.g., JSON mode, structured outputs)
actionable: Before fine-tuning, verify that frontier models cannot solve your task; plan fine-tuning only after exhausting simpler approaches (prompting, agents, workflows)
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Verifier's Law: the ease of training AI for a task is proportional to how easily verifiable that task is; tasks that are easier to verify are easier to apply reinforcement fine-tuning to
actionable: Prioritize fine-tuning for tasks with clear, codifiable success criteria; avoid fine-tuning for tasks where ground truth is subjective or hard to define
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Model context protocol (MCP) is a standard for connecting tools and context to LLM applications; standardization in larger orgs reduces duplication, improves onboarding, and enables consistent interfaces
actionable: If building tools for multiple LLM clients, expose them via MCP servers; if integrating many external tools, prefer MCP clients over custom integrations
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: Ambient (background) agents running async require careful human-in-the-loop design; implement checkpoints where agents notify users before critical actions or when stuck
actionable: Add gates for ambient agents: ping on questions, before major actions, on deadlock; implement memory so agents learn from feedback and adapt over time
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline

claim: As models get better at tool calling, agents become more feasible (models are doubling capability every 7 months); older coding agents fell into loops, but modern agents handle longer horizon tasks
actionable: Monitor tool-calling performance in latest models before rebuilding your agent harness; test that your agent doesn't fall into repeated tool loops
source_title: From Context Engineering to AI Agent Harnesses: The New Software Discipline
